topic: Advanced - const fn for Lookup Tables

turns.content: Hey, I've been reading up on using const fn to build lookup tables since my first day here, but honestly the stuff about perfect hashing and phf crates still feels like magic to me. I keep thinking I can just call a function with trigonometric values baked in at compile time without any runtime cost, but when I try it I get errors or weird behavior that makes no sense.

Hey! Don't worry, that's a very common hurdle. The misconception is thinking const fn can just magically return a complex calculated value without the data itself being pre-computed and stored in a specific structure. In Rust, if you want true zero-cost lookups for trig tables or perfect hashes, you typically need to combine a const fn with an array of u8 values representing the hash bits, which then gets passed into the phf crate's internal map at compile time. It isn't just calling a function; it is building a static table where every entry is a single byte derived from a const calculation.

Oh, so I'm not supposed to be calculating the sine of every angle on the fly inside a function? That sounds like what's breaking my code when I try to use it in a loop. It feels like there must be some kind of lazy evaluation trick going on that I just haven't figured out yet.

Exactly, and that is the key misunderstanding you need to resolve right now. Rust does not have lazy evaluation for data structures in this context; const fn computes values at compile time but cannot dynamically generate or resize arrays during execution. If your code looks like it is trying to build a table inside a function with conditional logic, that will fail because the compiler needs every single entry to be known before the program runs. You must pre-compute all sine and cosine pairs into a constant array of tuples using const fn, then index directly into that fixed-size structure at runtime.
Wait, so I can't just use an Option or a dynamic collection here? Like maybe there is some way to initialize it lazily with a trait bound that I'm missing?

No, for performance-critical game logic you absolutely cannot rely on lazy initialization or dynamic collections in the hot path. The phf crate works by taking a pre-computed perfect hash which is essentially a constant array of integers derived from const math, and it maps those directly to indices without any branching at runtime. There are no traits involved in the lookup itself; it is purely about ensuring every possible angle has a corresponding value stored in a u8 or usize array that can be indexed by integer arithmetic with zero overhead.

Okay, so I need to write a const fn that takes an angle and returns the index into a massive static array of pre-calculated floats? That seems like the only way to get this done without any runtime cost?

You are very close, but be precise: you write a const fn that computes the perfect hash for an angle and returns that index as usize. Then you access your pre-computed array of trigonometric values using that index at runtime. The magic is in the compile-time phase where every possible input maps to exactly one output byte or integer, eliminating any branching logic during actual gameplay execution.
